methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $26M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $110M - $200M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Graphyte
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $200M

private.name: Fourth Power
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $150M

private.name: Steady Energy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series A), post-money $110M

data.narrative:
Helios is a Series A climate and energy company founded by Jonathan Geifman and Dor Blum that has raised $26M to date. Valuation analysis draws on a peer set of 3 same-sector, same-stage private companies (Graphyte, Fourth Power, Steady Energy) alongside broader reference to 8 additional private frontier-tech peers and 1 public comparable (LNZA). The peer sample spans valuations from $110M to $200M.

Two triangulation methods apply. Stage-progression analysis applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier to the $26M raised proxy, implying $52M. Peer-median analysis across the 3 direct comparables yields a median valuation of $150M, with a range of $110M–$200M. These methods reflect different assumptions about capital intensity and market maturity within the climate tech segment.

The triangulated range supporting Helios is $44M (low) to $172M (high), with a median of $101M. The wide dispersion—driven by the 98 percentage-point gap between stage-progression and peer median results—reflects substantive uncertainty about whether the company's stage and capital efficiency aligns with peers or sits materially below. Resolution of this uncertainty requires clarity on revenue trajectory, customer concentration, and technical risk relative to the peer cohort. The $26M already deployed and stage placement argue against valuations near the peer maximum.
